summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his guest article for The Daily WTF (now Worse Than Failure), which ironically criticized the very site that invited him. While acknowledging the entertainment value of laughing at bad code, he argues that mockery alone perpetuates the problem. The developers who most need to improve will never read WTF, so the burden falls on skilled developers to actively mentor and reprogram their struggling colleagues. He calls for the software community to take collective responsibility through mentoring, code reviews, and peer pressure rather than passive amusement.
key_points: Laughing at bad code is therapeutic and educational, but entertainment alone doesn't solve the underlying problem
The developers who could benefit most from WTF will never read it—they don't know they need help
Waiting for bad developers to improve on their own is a strategy that never works
The software community has a collective duty to actively mentor and develop struggling programmers
Every developer, even great ones, writes bad code sometimes—the schism between 'good' and 'bad' programmers is false
money_quotes: I can absolutely guarantee that the kinds of developers who could benefit most from reading WTF simply do not – and never will – read the WTF website.
Reprogramming WTF code isn't enough. We need to reprogram the developers producing the WTF code.

I would fear them because they're either bald-faced liars – believing that simply saying it repeatedly will somehow convince others into this fiction – or they're completely blind to their own weaknesses.
I believe it's our collective duty to leave the profession of software engineering better than we found it.
Waiting for the bad developers to come to their senses on their own. That strategy never works.
It's tempting to get your daily fix of drive-by amusement and move on.
key_insight: Mocking bad code is cathartic but ultimately useless—the only way to reduce WTF-worthy code is for experienced developers to actively mentor those who write it.
